paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1184 English Language — AI-authored Simplified Practice 30: The Wet Market (§B style, simplified band)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a Secondary 2 girl who takes her younger brother to the wet market on a Sunday morning, loses sight of him in the crowd, searches in rising panic, and finally finds him at a stall. Answer the questions that follow. Section B [15 marks] — AI-authored simplified practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1184/02 (formerly 1128) Section B format and calibrated to the SEAB assessment objectives (literal / inferential comprehension, vocabulary in context, language for effect), pitched at students stretching from middle secondary toward the full O-Level standard. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. Items mix mark values [1]/[2]: 7 short-answer questions (Q1-Q7) plus a 4-blank emotion-flowchart MCQ (Q8). Answer in your own words as far as possible.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
Every Sunday my mother sends me to the wet market at the foot of our block in Ang Mo Kio with a long list and a red plastic basket. That morning she added one more thing: 'Take your brother along. And do not let go of his hand.' Wei Jie was six, small for his age, and he loved the market more than any playground.

Paragraph 2
The market was already loud and bright when we arrived at half past seven. Fishmongers shouted prices over silver heaps of ikan kuning. Water dripped from the wet floor onto my slippers. Wei Jie held my hand tightly, pointing at the live crabs that waved their claws in blue tubs, and I felt calm and in charge, the big sister doing her job.

Paragraph 3
We bought spinach, beancurd and a kilo of prawns. At the egg stall the auntie gave Wei Jie a free quail egg to hold, and he laughed. I let go of his hand for one second to count out the coins. One second. When I turned back, the space beside me was empty.

Paragraph 4
My stomach dropped. I spun around, calling his name, but my voice was swallowed by the noise of the crowd. Trolleys rolled past my knees. A tall man blocked my view. Everywhere there were legs, baskets and umbrellas, but no small boy in a yellow shirt. The aisle that had felt so familiar a moment ago now seemed enormous and strange.

Paragraph 5
I pushed through the wet walkways, my heart hammering against my ribs. I checked the fishmonger, the fruit corner, the place where they sold roast duck. I imagined Wei Jie wandering toward the busy road outside, or being led away by a stranger. My eyes stung. I had promised my mother. One job, and I had failed it in a single careless second.

Paragraph 6
Then, near the back of the market, I heard a familiar giggle. I followed it and stopped short. There was Wei Jie, perfectly safe, sitting on a low stool at the kueh stall. The kindly old uncle who sold the rainbow-coloured cakes had recognised him from our weekly visits and had simply waited with him. Wei Jie was happily eating a green ondeh-ondeh, his cheeks full.

Paragraph 7
'Jie Jie!' he cried, waving the half-eaten kueh at me. I did not know whether to scold him or hug him, so I did both. My legs felt weak as I crouched down and held him. The fear drained out of me, leaving behind a deep, warm wave of relief. The uncle only smiled and said, 'Next time, hold tighter.'

Paragraph 8
We walked home slowly, his sticky hand locked in mine, the basket heavier on my arm. I did not tell my mother the whole story at the door. But I understood now why she always reminded me twice. For the rest of that year, whenever we entered a crowd, I wound Wei Jie's hand into mine before he could even ask — and I never once let go to count coins again.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1184 §B style practice, simplified-band difficulty; not from a past exam. wet market: an open market selling fresh produce, fish and meat; ikan kuning: a small yellow-striped fish; Ang Mo Kio: a residential town in Singapore; kueh: traditional bite-sized cakes; ondeh-ondeh: a green glutinous-rice ball filled with palm sugar; Jie Jie: 'big sister' in Mandarin.)

questions.stem: Q1. From Paragraph 3, what was the narrator doing when she let go of Wei Jie's hand? [1]
questions.answer: counting out the coins to pay at the egg stall

questions.stem: Q2. What does the word 'swallowed' in 'my voice was swallowed by the noise of the crowd' (Paragraph 4) suggest about her calls for Wei Jie? [1]
questions.answer: they were completely drowned out / lost in the noise

questions.stem: Q3. What does the word 'hammering' (Paragraph 5) suggest about how the narrator's heart was beating? [1]
questions.answer: it was pounding hard and fast with fear / panic

questions.stem: Q4. Using your own words, explain why the familiar aisle now seemed 'enormous and strange' to the narrator. (Paragraph 4) [2]
questions.answer: ① her panic at losing Wei Jie changed the way she saw the market; ② the once-ordinary place now felt huge and frightening as she searched

questions.stem: Q5. Using your own words, explain the two fears that ran through the narrator's mind as she searched. (Paragraph 5) [2]
questions.answer: ① that Wei Jie might wander out toward the busy road; ② that he might be taken away by a stranger

questions.stem: Q6. Using your own words, explain why the narrator 'did not know whether to scold him or hug him, so I did both' (Paragraph 7). [2]
questions.answer: ① she was upset and frightened by the scare he had given her; ② yet relieved and loving because he was safe, so both feelings came out at once

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of the closing detail that she now winds Wei Jie's hand into hers 'before he could even ask' (Paragraph 8)? [2]
questions.answer: it shows she has learnt her lesson — the careless moment has made her protect her brother automatically, never repeating the mistake

sections.instruction: Q8. The narrator's main feeling shifts as the morning at the wet market unfolds. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes her dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: confident / frantic / relieved / wiser / proud / suspicious / annoyed / bored. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'The Wet Market' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift as the morning unfolds — from confident in Paragraph 2 when Wei Jie holds her hand tightly and she feels in charge as the capable big sister, to frantic in Paragraphs 4 and 5 when she turns to find him gone and pushes through the wet aisles with her heart hammering, imagining him near the road or led away by a stranger, to relieved in Paragraph 7 when she finds him safe at the kueh stall and holds him as the fear drains away, ending wiser in Paragraph 8 as she walks home and from then on grips his hand in every crowd before he can even ask. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q8(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 2, when they first arrive at the market, Wei Jie holds her hand tightly while pointing at the live crabs, and she feels calm and in charge as the big sister doing her job? [1]

Q8(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 4 and 5, when she turns to find the space beside her empty, spins around calling his name, and pushes through the wet walkways with her heart hammering, imagining him near the busy road or led away by a stranger? [1]

Q8(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 7, when she finds Wei Jie safe at the kueh stall, holds him as her legs feel weak, and the fear drains out of her, leaving a deep warm wave behind? [1]

Q8(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8, on the walk home and for the rest of that year, when she now winds Wei Jie's hand into hers before he can even ask and never again lets go to count coins? [1]
